A history-conditioned surrogate is then trained to map observed trajectories, candidate future locations, and responses to fast estimates of terminal and incremental information gain. This provides a cheap reward model for long-horizon planning. Building on this surrogate, we investigate two complementary planning mechanisms. First, a soft actor-critic policy is trained to make non-myopic path decisions under movement constraints. Second, a short-horizon model predictive control or reranking layer can refine the policy’s proposed actions using local surrogate scores, including predictive information gain and a complexity-aware term based on local curvature of the posterior predictive mean. This hybrid structure is intended to combine global long-horizon planning with local adaptive correction. The planned study compares the proposed SAC/MPC design strategy with greedy expected-information-gain policies and amortized design approaches inspired by Deep Adaptive Design. Preliminary synthetic examples are used to examine when non-greedy path planning provides advantages over one-step acquisition, especially in spatial fields with localized complex structure.

articles.title: mODa14
articles.contents:
The scientific workshops on model-oriented data analysis and optimum design (mODa) provide a high-level international forum for researchers, professionals and practitioners to present and discuss recent advances, new techniques and applications in the field of optimum experimental design. In addition, a primary aim is to provide young researchers an opportunity to establish personal contacts with leading specialists in the field. Its specificity is that the participation is by invitation of the board only.
mODa14 will take place from June 14 till June 19 2026 in Drongen Abbey, Belgium


Supported by

articles.title: mODa14
articles.contents:
The workshop is hosted by KU Leuven and takes place at the Old Abbey in Drongen, Belgium. Drongen Abbey is an established location for scientific conferences and has successfully hosted statistical meetings in the past, including in particular the Annual Meeting of the Royal Statistical Society (2024).
The location offers a main conference room with seating capacity of 110 participants, as well as opportunities to retreat for smaller working-group discussions. Drongen Abbey is easily accessible by public transportation from Gent (see travel), which is one of Belgium’s scientific and economic centres.






The abbey dates back to 1138 and was founded by the Norbertines. It was severely damaged on several occasions, notably during the Iconoclastic Fury of 1566. Rebuilt between 1638 and 1698, the abbey acquired much of its present appearance during that period.
The French Revolution forced the community to leave in 1796, and the property was subsequently sold at public auction. For a time, the site was used as a cotton factory, until the abbey buildings were purchased by the Jesuit order in 1836 and converted into a novitiate.
Since 1968, the abbey has served as a retreat and conference centre. The buildings and the surrounding grounds have been listed as a protected monument since 1998.








House Rules of the Old Abbey:
Respect the abbey’s atmosphere: Please keep the corridors and common areas quiet so everyone can enjoy the serene and inspiring environment of the abbey.
Quiet hours: Kindly observe quiet hours from 10:00 PM onwards.
Smoking policy: For your health and everyone’s safety, smoking is not allowed inside the buildings or courtyards. A designated smoking area is available by the parking lot.
Pets: To ensure hygiene and comfort for all guests, pets are not permitted on the premises.
Catering: All drinks and meals are provided by the Oude Abdij and should be arranged via the abbey.

Scientific Program
Stating the Problems: Design of Experiments in the
Pharmaceutical Industry - Valerii
Fedorov

The training will be interactive and filled with practical examples.
To bridge theory and practice, sessions will include software
demonstrations using both open-source and commercial tools.



Networking Opportunities
There will be plenty of time to connect during coffee breaks and the
networking dinner. The dinner will also be open to attendees arriving
for the mODa14 conference, offering a unique chance to engage with young
researchers and experienced academic experts in the field of Design of
Experiments.

The training is not-for-profit; all contributions will support mODa14
funding. Training materials are included in all options and will be
shared prior to the event.
Note: If you have specific design challenges you’d like
addressed during the training, please reach out. The organizers are
happy to demonstrate their expertise and enthusiasm on real-world
problems.

articles.contents:
Address:Old Abbey, Drongenplein 27, 9031 Drongen (Ghent), Belgium
Getting from Ghent Sint-Pieters Station to the Old Abbey
The Old Abbey is located just 5 kilometers from Ghent and is easily accessible by public transport. On the map below, the route by train is shown in red, and the route by bus in orange.




By train:
On Sundays, there is unfortunately only one train per hour (at xx:43) from Ghent Sint-Pieters to Drongen station. The journey takes approximately 5 minutes and costs 3 to 5 euros. This is a local train with final destination Brugge (Zeebrugge). The Old Abbey is a 750-meter walk from Drongen station.
By bus:
(Subject to change, as schedules are updated at the end of May)
Bus 50 runs on Sundays every hour at xx:19 from platform 14 in front of Ghent Sint-Pieters to Drongen Rotonde, a 500-meter walk from the Old Abbey. The cost of 3 euros can be paid on the bus via Maestro, Mastercard, VPAY, Visa, Apple Pay, Google Pay, Fitbit Pay, or Garmin Pay.
By taxi:
It takes approximately 7 minutes by taxi from Ghent Station to the Old Abbey. Expected cost is around 20 euros.

Getting to Ghent Sint-Pieters Station
By plane to Brussels Airport and then by train to Ghent:
The closest airport to Ghent is Brussels National Airport (BRU), also known as Zaventem, 67 kilometers from Ghent. There are several direct flights per day from major European and North-American cities. The train station is located underneath the airport and accessible via lifts and escalators in the arrivals hall. The departure station is called Brussel Nationaal Luchthaven / Brussels National Airport. The destination station is Gent Sint-Pieters. Tickets can be bought at the train station for about 15 euros per person, one-way.
On Sundays, there is only 1 direct train per hour to Ghent at xx:24, which takes 55 minutes. The final destination of this IC train usually is Oostende, Knokke, or De Panne. You can also take one of the other two trains per hour, but these require changing trains in one of the three Brussels stations (Bruxelles Nord / Brussel Noord, Bruxelles Central / Brussel Centraal, or Bruxelles Midi / Brussel Zuid) to connect to an intercity (IC) train to Ghent.
Taxi note: Taxis are available outside the airport building but are very expensive in Belgium. A ride from Brussels Airport to Ghent or Drongen costs between 175 and 250 euros.
Other convenient airports: Amsterdam Schiphol (225 km / 140 mi) and Paris Charles de Gaulle / Roissy (270 km / 167 mi). Both airports have (high-speed) train connections to Ghent Sint-Pieters station. Tickets can be booked via the Belgian, French, or Dutch railway websites. Enter Gent Sint-Pieters as your destination.

Travelling by car
If you are travelling by car, please use the address above for navigation. The Old Abbey offers approximately 35 on-site parking spaces. Alternatively, two free public parking lots are available at Drongen railway station nearby.
